title: Federated Generalised Variational Inference: A Robust Probabilistic Federated Learning Framework

reviews.summary: The paper proposes FEDGVI, a federated learning framework that replaces classical Bayesian updating with Generalised Variational Inference (GVI) on both clients and server to achieve robustness under prior and likelihood misspecification. The authors show that FEDGVI recovers prior work (PVI, VI, and even FedAvg) as special cases, provide theoretical results on fixed points, externally Bayesian logarithmic opinion pooling via damping, the necessity of the cavity distribution, closed-form conjugate client updates under score-matching losses, and a robustness-to-outliers theorem. Empirically, across synthetic and real classification tasks (logistic regression, CoverType, MNIST, FashionMNIST) and several baselines (PVI, FedAvg, DSGLD, FedPA, DSVGD, β-PREDBAYES), FEDGVI demonstrates improved robustness to contamination and competitive or better predictive performance.
strengths: Introduces a principled federated GVI framework that unifies and generalizes PVI/VI/FedAvg through the choice of loss, divergence, and variational family.
Formalises the role of damping at the server as an externally Bayesian logarithmic opinion pool (Proposition 4.3), offering a clean interpretation of a commonly heuristic choice in prior work.

Proves that, under specific settings, FEDGVI recovers the GBI posterior (Lemma 4.6), and establishes a fixed-point characterization as a minimizer of a global GVI objective (Proposition 4.4), extending PVI theory to general losses.
Provides a compelling necessity result for the cavity distribution (Theorem 4.9) in the federated GVI setting.
Derives conditions for conjugate client updates using weighted score matching to obtain closed-form Gaussian updates (Proposition 4.10), increasing practical tractability.
States a robustness-to-outliers theorem at the server level under robust client losses (Theorem 4.12), connecting local robust losses to global robustness.
Includes synthetic stress tests (clutter problem, logistic regression with outliers) isolating robustness properties.

Real dataset evaluations (CoverType, MNIST, FashionMNIST) with contamination demonstrate improved robustness and accuracy versus competitive baselines, including both Bayesian and frequentist FL methods.
Ablations over α-Rényi and generalized cross-entropy (GCE) parameters support stability over a range of hyperparameters.
The method and algorithms are clearly described, with a coherent mapping to known inference paradigms and careful notation.
Theoretical results are stated precisely with references to appendices for proofs; relationships to prior work are summarized via a helpful “recovery” diagram.

Robustness to model misspecification is a central and under-addressed challenge in FL; providing a general, theoretically grounded framework that is compatible with standard FL pipelines is timely and impactful.
The analysis of damping as a principled opinion pool and the necessity of the cavity could influence future federated Bayesian/variational methods beyond this work.
weaknesses: The main robustness theorem (Theorem 4.12) assumes Q = P(Θ) (unrestricted variational family), bounded prior, and lower-bounded losses; practical implementations typically use restricted parametric families (e.g., mean-field Gaussians), and it is unclear how much of the robustness transfers under such approximations.

Strong claims in the abstract, such as “unbiased predictions under model misspecification,” appear broader than what is theoretically established (the proven property is robustness to outliers/contamination under specific assumptions; general unbiasedness is not shown).

General convergence guarantees for the single-loop algorithm remain limited (similar to PVI/EP), beyond fixed-point characterizations; stability still hinges on damping heuristics or pooling interpretations.

Most experiments use homogeneous client splits; robustness to typical FL heterogeneities (label/feature skew, unbalanced client sizes, partial participation, asynchronous updates) is not comprehensively evaluated despite claims of easy extensibility.
Calibration is a key motivation, but calibration metrics (e.g., ECE, Brier score, coverage) are not reported; only accuracy/NLL are provided on real tasks.

Hyperparameter selection for robust losses (β, γ, δ, α) is nontrivial; while ablations are given, more guidance (or automated selection) and sensitivity in federated, heterogeneous settings would be valuable.

Communication and runtime comparisons are only partially discussed; inclusion of wall-clock, bandwidth, and memory profiles against strong Bayesian FL baselines would strengthen claims of practicality.
Some proofs depend on nontrivial assumptions (e.g., bounded priors/losses) that are not discussed in the main text for common models; practical guidance on ensuring these hold is limited.
The notion of “regularization by the cavity” in Theorem 4.9 could be more explicitly formalized in the main text for broader accessibility.

Recent EP-style federated methods (e.g., scalable FedEP variants) are cited but not included as baselines; since FEDGVI conceptually extends PVI/EP ideas with robust losses/divergences, a direct empirical comparison would contextualize gains.

There is limited discussion of calibration-focused Bayesian FL under strict bandwidth (e.g., compressed particle-based methods); given the claimed calibration benefits, a brief empirical or conceptual comparison would help.
reviews.questions:
1. The robustness theorem assumes Q = P(Θ). Do you have theoretical or empirical insights on how robustness degrades under restricted variational families (e.g., mean-field Gaussians for BNNs)? Can any partial guarantees be established?
2. The abstract claims “unbiased predictions under model misspecification.” Can you clarify the precise sense of unbiasedness you intend? Is this meant in the sense of bounded influence/robustness rather than unbiasedness in expectation?
3. How sensitive is performance to δ (GCE), β/γ (divergence-based robust losses), and α (Rényi) under strongly non-IID client splits? Could you provide results on a standard non-IID FL benchmark (e.g., label distribution skew) to complement the current homogeneous-split experiments?
4. Could you report uncertainty calibration metrics (ECE, Brier, coverage) under varying contamination levels and client heterogeneity? Since calibration is a stated motivation, this would substantiate the claim of calibrated uncertainty.
5. How does FEDGVI compare to FedEP-style methods empirically on at least one benchmark (with or without contamination)? Even if FedEP is not explicitly robust, it is a strong Bayesian FL baseline.
6. What are the compute/memory/communication overheads relative to PVI and DSGLD/FedPA in your BNN experiments (per-round bandwidth, client memory for sites, and wall-clock)?
7. Theorem 4.9: can you make “the cavity regularises the client optimisation” more explicit in the main text (e.g., equivalent optimality conditions), and illustrate with a concrete counterexample when not using the cavity?
reviews.assessment: This paper presents a timely and well-executed contribution: a principled robust probabilistic FL framework that generalizes prior Bayesian/variational approaches, accompanied by clear theoretical insights (opinion pooling via damping, fixed-point optimality for general losses, necessity of the cavity) and a robustness guarantee under contamination, plus a practical path to conjugate updates via score matching. The empirical evidence is strong for robustness on synthetic and several real datasets, and the ablations help establish hyperparameter stability. The main limitations are (i) the gap between theoretical robustness (for unrestricted Q) and practical variational families, (ii) a lack of evaluation under standard non-IID/partial participation settings, and (iii) missing calibration metrics and comparison to recent EP-style federated baselines. These are addressable: adding non-IID experiments, calibration results, overhead analyses, and (ideally) a FedEP comparison would substantively strengthen the submission. Overall, the paper advances the state of robust Bayesian FL and would be valuable to the ICML community; I lean positive, contingent on bolstering empirical breadth and clarifying the scope of the theoretical guarantees relative to practical approximations.
